name: Bank for International Settlements
description: The Bank for International Settlements (BIS) is an international financial institution owned by central banks, fostering monetary and financial stability and serving as a bank for central banks. Established in 1930, its original purpose was to manage German reparations payments after World War I. Today, it facilitates cooperation among central banks, conducts research, and acts as a forum for policy dialogue. Some conspiracy theories allege the BIS is a secretive, unelected body controlling global finance and manipulating national economies for the benefit of a shadowy elite. Proponents point to the BIS's influence on central bank policies and its closed-door meetings as evidence of its hidden agenda. They believe it aims to establish a one-world currency and government, eroding national sovereignty. Mainstream perspectives view the BIS as a crucial institution for global financial stability, providing a platform for central banks to coordinate policies and address common challenges. Academics emphasize its role in preventing financial crises and promoting international monetary cooperation. Critics acknowledge the BIS's influence but argue it operates with a degree of transparency and accountability, subject to oversight by its member central banks. They dismiss claims of a hidden agenda as unfounded, emphasizing the BIS's stated goals of promoting financial stability and international cooperation. The BIS publishes annual reports and working papers that are publicly available.
subtitle: Central bank cooperation or global control?

claims.claim: The BIS is a secretive organization controlling global finance.
claims.evidence: Closed-door meetings, lack of public transparency in certain operations, influence on central bank policies.
claims.criticism: The BIS operates under a charter agreed upon by its member central banks and publishes annual reports. Its activities are subject to scrutiny by these banks and international organizations.

claims.claim: The BIS aims to establish a one-world currency and government.
claims.evidence: Statements by some BIS officials interpreted as advocating for greater international cooperation, perceived erosion of national sovereignty due to BIS-influenced policies.
claims.criticism: The BIS's stated goals are to promote financial stability and international monetary cooperation, not to establish a world government or currency. Its recommendations are non-binding.

claims.claim: The BIS manipulates national economies for the benefit of a shadowy elite.
claims.evidence: Correlation between BIS policies and economic outcomes, perceived influence of powerful individuals on the BIS's agenda.
claims.criticism: Economic outcomes are influenced by numerous factors, and attributing them solely to the BIS is an oversimplification. The BIS's policies are based on economic analysis and consensus among its member central banks.

keyFigures.name: Gabriel Makhlouf
keyFigures.role: Chairman of the BIS Board of Directors

keyFigures.name: Hyun Song Shin
keyFigures.role: Economic Adviser and Head of Research at the BIS

keyFigures.name: Carroll Quigley
keyFigures.role: Historian whose work 'Tragedy and Hope' is cited by some to support conspiracy theories about the BIS's influence

mythologyConnections: The concept of a hidden, powerful group controlling the world echoes themes found in various mythologies, such as the Illuminati or other secret societies.
Connections to the idea of a 'world order' as depicted in certain prophetic texts.

timeline.event: The Bank for International Settlements is established.
The BIS plays a role in managing the European Payments Union.
The BIS continues to serve as a forum for central bank cooperation and research on global financial issues.

sources.author: Adam LeBor
sources.work: Tower of Basel: The Shadowy History of the Secret Bank That Runs the World
sources.title: Adam LeBor

sources.author: Gianni Toniolo
sources.work: Central Bank Cooperation at the Bank for International Settlements, 1930-1973
sources.title: Gianni Toniolo
